payload.text:
Necesito crear una página web o landing page de un grupo de psicólogos que se llama Grupo Faro. Es un grupo de psicólogos, psiquiatras y profesionales de la salud que funcionan en red para atender pacientes presenciales y online, personas, hombres, mujeres de distintas edades, principalmente adolescentes y adultos.

En donde se explique, el foco de la atención, distintas secciones, incluyendo una sección donde se pueda contactar directamente con los psicólogos, donde se puedan hacer preguntas también. Otras secciones donde se vaya entregando contenido e información.

Y va a haber otra donde se presentan los principales directivos de este grupo, más otros profesionales.

Debe ser en un contenido más o menos adecuado a lo que es una página que entregaría un servicio psicológico, con íconos adecuados a eso y ojalá con una ambientación que genere tranquilidad, paz, etc.

**Considering the Core Purpose**

My focus is now firmly on the initial goal: building a compelling landing page for Grupo Faro. I'm actively considering user needs and how to best showcase their services. Emphasis is on clear communication of Grupo Faro's value proposition.


**Refining the Blueprint**

I'm now diving into the specifics of the landing page's structure and components. I've broken down `App.tsx`, the `Header`, `Hero`, `Services`, `Team`, and other crucial sections. The goal is to craft a visually soothing and professional interface that also clearly highlights Grupo Faro's offerings to both adolescents and adults. I'm thinking of how to use Tailwind CSS for rapid prototyping.


**Planning Feature Integration**

I've outlined several more sections: a blog for educational content, a standard contact form, and a chat assistant powered by Gemini. I'm focusing on user interaction flow now.
I'm exploring the nuances of incorporating a Gemini-powered Q&A feature to offer preliminary advice, along with a footer for essential links. The design will be key. I've chosen Tailwind CSS and identified a suitable color palette for a clinical yet inviting look. The blog will support engagement. The chat assistant is a powerful addition to the initial plan. I'm thinking about how to integrate Gemini with the chat assistant.


**Constructing the Architecture**

I'm deep into the coding phase now, building the application's components. I've defined data structures in `types.ts` and set up constants for mock data. The Gemini service integration is underway, now using the `@google/genai` syntax.  I've started on the core UI components like the Navbar, Hero, and AboutServices section. I'm focusing on responsiveness and smooth user flow, especially for mobile users.


**Developing the Components**

I've got the foundational components sorted. I've built the `Team.tsx`, `ContentSection.tsx`, `ContactForm.tsx`, and `GeminiChat.tsx` components.  I've chosen an embedded section for the chat near the contact form.  I'm now integrating everything in `App.tsx`. The goal is a clean Spanish translation with empathetic language and a lighthouse theme.  No need for camera/mic functionality at this stage.


**Assessing Feature Placement**

I'm evaluating whether to integrate the Gemini-powered chat assistant as a dedicated section or a floating widget. A dedicated section prioritizes SEO, while a floating widget offers a modern, interactive feel. For simplicity, the landing page will focus on text-based interaction, with no camera/microphone integration unless requested. I've re-reviewed the code against the project rules, confirming compliance with React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind, and Gemini SDK.


**Enhancing Interactive Features**

I'm now thinking about how to integrate the Gemini chat as a dedicated "Preguntas Frecuentes con IA" (Interactive Q&A) section to draw attention to it. This section will be prominently featured on the landing page. It's crucial this element stands out as a key benefit for users.
All required code files are included in the single XML block below.

payload.text: In `components/Navbar.tsx`, implement smooth scrolling for the navigation links to smoothly scroll to the corresponding sections when clicked.

**Improving Scroll Behavior**

I'm currently focused on implementing smooth scrolling within the `Navbar.tsx` component. The initial approach involved standard anchor tags with `href="#id"`, but I'm exploring enhancements beyond that. While browser-level `scroll-behavior: smooth` is an option, it's not the ultimate solution here. I'm keen to offer a more customized and consistent experience across browsers.


**Investigating Smooth Scrolling Implementation**

I've been examining the `index.html` file and discovered that `scroll-behavior: smooth` is applied to the `body` element rather than the `html` element, which is the more common practice. I'm considering the potential impact of this on the implementation within `Navbar.tsx`, where I'm aiming for a custom JavaScript-driven smooth scrolling solution, potentially to handle cases where browser defaults or React Router might interfere. I'm focusing on offset calculations for the fixed navbar.


**Crafting a JavaScript Solution**

The anchor jumps hide the section title behind the fixed header. I need a JS handler to calculate and apply an offset to the scroll position. I'm developing a `handleNavClick` function within `Navbar.tsx` that will intercept the link clicks, prevent the default behavior, locate the target element, and then scroll to it with an offset, accounting for the navbar's height. This height, however, fluctuates, but a rough estimate is around 80-90px. I'm now writing the detailed specification.


**Integrating Smooth Scroll Functionality**

I've integrated the `handleNavClick` function within `Navbar.tsx` to manage smooth scrolling. The core logic involves preventing default anchor behavior, identifying the target element, calculating the offset (accounting for the fixed header), and then employing `window.scrollTo({ top: offsetPosition, behavior: 'smooth' })`. I've also added the function to mobile menu links and the "Agendar Hora" button, offering a uniform experience. Now, the implementation is nearly complete, and testing will follow shortly.


**Testing Smooth Scroll Updates**

I'm now testing the newly implemented smooth scrolling across various screen sizes and devices. I've integrated `handleNavClick` with the mobile menu links and the main navigation. I'm keen to catch any edge cases or unexpected behaviors related to different screen resolutions or browser quirks. I'm now integrating the JavaScript solution to handle smooth scrolling within `Navbar.tsx`. The goal is to ensure a seamless scrolling experience, particularly when navigating between sections.
